text: Hi, welcome to Dizima Architecture. My name is Andrea and I'm here with Vipul from ADP.
Hi Vipul, thanks for being here. Hi Andrea, how are you? I'm good, thank you. So what do you guys do?
So at ADP we're one of the country's largest providers, actually one of the world's largest
providers of HR systems and payroll. Great, so we're here to talk about Workforce Now,
which sits on AWS. Yes. So tell our viewers what is that? So ADP Workforce Now is our human resource
HR and human capital management and payroll system. We've got benefits, we've got talent
management, we've got recruiting, as we say from hire to retire and it is our solution that's used
in the mid-market. It goes from some very small clients all the way up to enterprise size. Great,
let's dive into the architecture. I see lots of different services and so you have users I'm sure,
right? Employees and I see some mobile devices and computer systems and ELB. How do you access the system?
So coming, you know, we have an API layer that requests, all requests come in, they go through our load balancer
cluster and then right into our EKS cluster. Okay, so this must be heart of this whole architecture,
right? The application logic, tell us what size EKS cluster you're running. Well, it's, it's, we have
hundreds of microservices that are running inside this EKS cluster and those microservices are grouped
together in node clusters. Because depending on what part of the application you're in, there are
different characteristics of that. In addition to that, one of the services we run in here is our continuous
calculation real-time payroll engine. Okay, so it depends obviously me, myself, you know, logging into my system.
Yep. I might do it early in the day. I'm sure you have seasonality and there's also time of day. How do you scale up and down?
So again, as you just said, we have seasonality during the year for like, let's say, benefits annual enrollment.
You know, October, November are the busy months when people go do that. It can happen other times in the
year too. But that's traditionally busy time. So we scale up those benefits services during that period
of time. But on a daily basis, we have people clocking in and clocking out to start and end their shift.
And so those services scale up on a daily basis and then they scale back down again when when traffic is low.
Oh, that makes perfect sense for performance and cost, I'm sure. So I see streaming for Kafka. What do you use that for?
Okay. So let's take the clocking out example. So when someone clocks out, right, an event is dropped onto Kafka.
That event is read by our continuous calc payroll engine. And once that clock out event is read, immediately for that user,
their payroll up to the minute for that day is calculated. In addition to that, at the company level, all the liabilities,
because it's a continuous real time payroll, all those liabilities of the company level are known as well.
Okay. So is event management at high speed?
It is. Yes. And you do this to keep obviously information current and available real time. Where do you store this information?
So all the information is stored in Aurora. That's our system of record.
Okay.
We're using Postgres on Aurora.
Okay.
Once that information is stored in there, we sync it back up with Elastic Cache and with Elastic Search as well.
So again, like when someone logs in, right, the first thing when they log into these services, we go over here to our cache service
and saying, hey, what are you entitled to? And we bring back and retrieve the data that user, and by the way,
that user could be an employee, could be a manager, or it could be what we call a practitioner, what data that user needs readily.
So you make this information readily available quickly through Elastic Cache.
Yes.
And then Elastic Search.
Yes.
Are you using Elastic Search?
We're using Elastic Search too.
Okay.
So Elastic Search, again, back to whether it's the employees or the employers, right, there's various different bits of information they want to look up.
As an employer, I may want to look at all my employees.
As an employee, I may want to look at my payroll history, let's say, or other history.
So we use Elastic Search to go get that data and serve it back up to the clients.
This is very sensitive information.
It is.
I'm sure no one wants to have access to anyone else's data.
How do you secure it in Aurora?
All the security in Aurora is done at the row level.
So we do row level security, and by the way, encryption as well too.
In fact, the entire environment for all the data that's in transit or at rest is fully encrypted.
Yeah, that's very impressive.
And also, let's talk about geographic, you know, say the regions you're using.
I'm sure you have users all around the world.
What's the geographic presence?
Yeah, so we're using multi-availability zones, multi-region throughout US and throughout Canada, right?
I see.
And in fact, our system, our workforce now today, has about 70,000 clients on it with tens of millions of users.
So everything that we've architected over here is to handle that load.
And as we're, you know, moving our clients and putting them live on this, we're ready for that load.
And that's what was architected for, was to handle those loads.
And quite frankly, it'll scale even larger.
Yeah.
Very interesting architecture.

You walked us through, you know, the Workforce Now platform, which is highly scalable and, you know, handling all the application logic with EKS, as well as event management at high speed with Kafka, and then storing, making data accessible, and encrypting data for users.
using RDS, as well as Elastic Lass and Elasticsearch.
So thanks for walking us through this architecture.
Thank you for having me today.
Yeah.
And thank you for watching.
This is my architecture.
